refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle time performance against customer-defined targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure and report cycle time data, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The latter is an acceptance criterion (a constraint on the system's behavior under specified conditions), not a functional requirement the system must actively 'support' in design. The refined FR focuses on the functional obligationâ€”providing visibility into cycle timeâ€”which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the target (p95 â‰¤ CT_target) is then verified as a constraint satisfaction check, not as a separate FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system captures and reports cycle time for each loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle with sufficient granularity and accuracy to enable statistical analysis (e.g., p95 calculation). Verify that reported cycle times are traceable to actual field operations under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Confirm that the measurement system does not introduce systematic bias or loss of data.
sub_frs: Capture cycle phase timestamps (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end) with sufficient precision and accuracy.
Aggregate and store cycle time data for each completed loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle.
Provide access to cycle time data for analysis and reporting (e.g., to Site Manager, Project Manager, System Engineer).
Enable filtering and statistical analysis of cycle time data by duty cycle, site conditions, and time period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument load-bearing structure and dump mechanism with displacement/force transducers; aggregate raw signals into a time-series event log; apply threshold-crossing logic to detect cycle phase boundaries (load pickup, travel start, dump completion); compute elapsed time between phase transitions; compare p95 aggregate against CT_target via statistical analysis module.

DP2: Deploy discrete event sensors (binary switches/proximity detectors) at fixed cycle checkpoints (load engaged, vehicle departed, dump gate opened, dump gate closed); timestamp each event; reconstruct cycle duration from event sequence; filter outliers and compute p95 percentile; report pass/fail against CT_target.

DP3: Integrate continuous position/velocity telemetry from vehicle propulsion and hydraulic actuation systems; fuse multi-source signals into a unified motion model; apply state-machine logic to infer cycle phases; extract phase transition times; aggregate into cycle time distribution; compute p95 and trend analysis.

DP4: Establish operator-initiated manual checkpoint logging (start/end timestamps recorded by site personnel via mobile or fixed interface); aggregate logged intervals; compute cycle time statistics; compare against CT_target with manual audit trail for validation.

DP5: Combine sparse event detection (DP2 approach) with continuous telemetry (DP3 approach) in a hybrid architecture: use events for phase confirmation and telemetry for fine-grained timing refinement; fuse both streams to improve robustness and reduce false-phase detection.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid event-telemetry fusion architecture. Deploy discrete event sensors at critical cycle boundaries (load engagement, travel initiation, dump completion) to establish phase checkpoints with high confidence. Simultaneously acquire continuous position/velocity signals from vehicle and hydraulic subsystems. Implement a state-machine inference engine that uses event timestamps as phase anchors and telemetry as refinement data to resolve ambiguous transitions. Aggregate fused cycle times into a rolling statistical buffer; compute p95 percentile and compare against CT_target. Maintain an audit trail linking each measured cycle to raw sensor data and inferred phase sequence for traceability and root-cause analysis of outliers.

potential_sources: Event sensor placement is tightly coupled to vehicle/dump mechanism designâ€”sensor mounting points and trigger thresholds must be co-designed with mechanical engineering; mitigation: define sensor interface specifications (mounting envelope, signal type, response time) early and decouple sensor logic from mechanical geometry.

Telemetry signal quality depends on vehicle propulsion and hydraulic system instrumentation maturity; if those subsystems lack native telemetry, retrofitting becomes costly; mitigation: establish telemetry availability as a design assumption and negotiate with vehicle/subsystem suppliers.

State-machine logic is coupled to the specific sequence of loadâ†’travelâ†’dump operations; changes to operational procedure (e.g., partial dumps, multi-stop routes) require state-machine revision; mitigation: parameterize state transitions and allow operational policy updates without code changes.
p95 computation and CT_target comparison logic is independent of sensor choice and can be decoupled into a separate reporting module; low coupling risk here.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves good separation of concerns: event detection (binary, robust), telemetry fusion (continuous, refined), and statistical analysis (policy-agnostic) are logically distinct. Hybrid approach reduces single-point-of-failure risk compared to DP2 or DP3 alone. Main coupling risk is mechanical integration; recommend early co-design with vehicle platform team.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Event sensors (e.g., proximity switches, load cells) can be reliably mounted on the vehicle and dump mechanism without interfering with normal operation. Risk: Harsh worksite environment (dust, vibration, thermal cycling) may degrade sensor reliability; mitigation: specify industrial-grade sensors with appropriate IP rating and conduct field durability testing.

Assumption: Vehicle propulsion and hydraulic systems provide accessible telemetry signals (CAN bus, analog outputs, or equivalent). Risk: Legacy vehicles may lack native telemetry; retrofitting is expensive and may void warranties. Mitigation: confirm telemetry availability with vehicle supplier; consider aftermarket telemetry retrofit as fallback.

Assumption: Cycle phases (load, travel, dump) are always sequential and non-overlapping under normal operation. Risk: Operator error or equipment malfunction (e.g., premature dump during travel) may violate phase assumptions; mitigation: add anomaly detection logic to flag out-of-sequence events and alert operators.

Assumption: CT_target is stable and well-defined by customer. Risk: Customer may not have historical baseline data or may set unrealistic targets; mitigation: conduct baseline measurement campaign before acceptance testing to establish realistic p95 distribution.

Assumption: Worksite conditions (road surface, traffic, weather) remain consistent during measurement period. Risk: Variability in site conditions may inflate p95 beyond vehicle capability; mitigation: stratify measurements by site condition and document environmental context in reporting.

Assumption: p95 percentile is the appropriate acceptance metric. Risk: Single-point metric may mask bimodal or heavy-tailed distributions; mitigation: include full distribution visualization and trend analysis in reporting to support root-cause investigation.

Assumption: Measurement system can be deployed and maintained by site personnel without specialized training. Risk: Complex sensor calibration or data fusion logic may require expert support; mitigation: design for simplicity and provide clear operational procedures and troubleshooting guides.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) measurement granularity (per-cycle vs. aggregate statistics), (2) reporting frequency (real-time, daily, weekly), (3) acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., Â±2% cycle time), (4) data retention and audit trail requirements, and (5) failure modes and fallback behavior if sensors fail. Clarify whether CT_target is a hard acceptance criterion or a performance baseline for continuous improvement. Establish a baseline measurement campaign (DP2 or DP4 only) before committing to full DP5 deployment to validate assumptions about cycle variability and sensor feasibility. Define state-machine logic and phase transition thresholds as a separate design artifact (DP specification) to enable independent testing and operational policy updates.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized hybrid event-telemetry fusion engine that ingests discrete event signals from vehicle subsystems, correlates them with continuous telemetry streams, executes state-machine phase inference, maintains rolling cycle-time statistics, and generates audit trails for traceability and outlier root-cause analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Continuous position, velocity, and hydraulic subsystem signals from Vehicle; discrete event timestamps (load engagement, travel initiation, dump completion) from Vehicle sensors.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: CT_target cycle-time threshold and statistical aggregation parameters (e.g., p95 percentile window size) from operational control layer.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Alerts triggered when measured p95 cycle time exceeds CT_target; linked to audit trail for root-cause investigation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmission of fused cycle metrics, statistical summaries, and audit trail records to remote monitoring or persistence layer; reception of configuration updates or threshold adjustments.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for fusion logic, state-machine inference, and statistical aggregation. Event-telemetry correlation, phase anchoring, and audit trail generation are core to a guidance/health/efficiency platform. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces are telemetry ingestion (from Vehicle), tasking parameters (from control), and alert/reporting outputs (to Safety Services and Network). No algorithmic leakage into other modules.

Poor fit. Vehicle module should own sensor hardware and raw signal acquisition, not fusion logic or state inference. Placing DP5 here would scatter the fusion engine across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling to vehicle architecture and making the algorithm difficult to update, test, or reuse. Audit trail and statistical aggregation would be orphaned from the inference logic.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a user-facing control interface, not a data fusion or analytics platform. Placing DP5 here would conflate operator commands with backend telemetry processing, creating high coupling and poor separation of concerns. State-machine inference and audit trails belong in a backend service, not in a teleoperation client.

Weak fit. Safety Services should consume alerts and enforce safety constraints, not own the fusion and statistical inference engine. While SafetyAlert is an output interface, the core DP5 logic (event-telemetry fusion, state-machine inference, rolling statistics) is not a safety function per se; it is a performance/health monitoring function that feeds safety decisions. Placing DP5 here would overload Safety Services with non-safety analytics.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal boundary for DP5 because it is architecturally positioned as a backend analytics and guidance platform. It naturally owns telemetry fusion, state inference, and performance metrics aggregation. The module has clear input interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking) and output interfaces (SafetyAlert, Network Link), enabling clean separation from Vehicle hardware, Teleoperation UI, and Safety Services decision logic. The audit trail and statistical buffer are internal to the module and support its core mission of health and efficiency monitoring.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle sensors are not reliably time-synchronized, event timestamp anchoring may fail. Mitigation: Establish a time-sync protocol between Vehicle and NGHE Solution at the Telemetry interface.

Risk: If state-machine inference logic becomes overly complex (many cycle phases, edge cases), the module may become difficult to maintain and test. Mitigation: Decompose state machine into smaller, testable sub-modules within NGHE Solution; document phase transition rules and validation criteria.

Risk: Audit trail storage and retrieval could become a bottleneck if cycle frequency is high and retention period is long. Mitigation: Implement efficient circular buffer or time-windowed persistence; consider offloading historical audit data to Network Link for archival.

Risk: If CT_target or statistical parameters are updated frequently via Tasking interface, the rolling buffer may need to be reset or recalibrated. Mitigation: Define clear semantics for parameter updates (e.g., apply to next cycle, drain current buffer, or merge with existing statistics).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 â‰¤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via the NGHE Solution's rolling statistical buffer and audit trail. The IM boundary provides clear traceability: each measured cycle is linked to raw sensor data and inferred phase sequence, enabling root-cause analysis of outliers and validation against CT_target. Verification can be performed by extracting p95 percentile from the statistical aggregation module and comparing against the tasking parameter CT_target.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR is unambiguously allocated to NGHE Solution as the sole owner of the hybrid event-telemetry fusion engine. The IM boundary is well-defined with four explicit external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout), each with clear data contracts. No ambiguity exists regarding which module owns fusion logic, state-machine inference, statistical aggregation, or audit trail generation. Vehicle owns sensor acquisition; Safety Services consumes alerts; Teleoperation remains a UI client. Ownership is singular and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (Hybrid event-telemetry fusion architecture) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP specifies: (1) discrete event sensors at cycle boundaries to establish phase checkpoints, (2) continuous telemetry acquisition for refinement, (3) state-machine inference to resolve ambiguous transitions, (4) rolling statistical aggregation with p95 percentile computation, and (5) audit trail linking cycles to raw data and inferred phases. These mechanisms collectively enable measurement and reporting of loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle time performance against CT_target. The DP is logically sound, complete, and traceable to the FR.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Event timestamp synchronization. If Vehicle sensors lack reliable time-sync with NGHE Solution, event anchoring will fail. Mitigation: Enforce time-sync protocol at Telemetry interface; validate timestamp coherence in state-machine initialization.

Risk: State-machine complexity creep. If cycle phases proliferate or edge cases accumulate, inference logic becomes difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: Decompose state machine into smaller, independently testable sub-modules within NGHE Solution; maintain formal phase transition documentation.

Risk: Audit trail storage bottleneck. High cycle frequency and long retention periods may cause I/O or memory pressure. Mitigation: Implement circular buffer with time-windowed persistence; offload historical data to Network Link for archival.

Risk: Parameter update semantics ambiguity. Frequent CT_target or statistical parameter updates via Tasking interface may leave rolling buffer in inconsistent state. Mitigation: Define explicit update semantics (e.g., apply to next cycle, drain current buffer, or merge with existing statistics); document in Tasking interface contract.
Risk: Minimal diffusion observed (0.08 score). No algorithmic leakage detected across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. DP5 is well-contained within NGHE Solution boundary.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with only 0.08 diffusion. The module is architecturally positioned as a backend analytics platform and naturally owns telemetry fusion, state inference, and performance metrics aggregation. All core DP5 responsibilities (event-telemetry correlation, phase anchoring, statistical aggregation, audit trail generation) reside within a single module boundary. External interfaces are clean and minimal: Telemetry ingestion (from Vehicle), Tasking parameters (from control layer), SafetyAlert output (to Safety Services), and Network Link (for reporting/persistence). No DP5 logic is scattered across Vehicle firmware, Teleoperation UI, or Safety Services. The module has clear ownership and well-defined responsibilities.

recommended_revisions: Establish and document a time-synchronization protocol between Vehicle sensors and NGHE Solution at the Telemetry interface. Define acceptable clock skew tolerances and validation checks to ensure event timestamp reliability.

Decompose the state-machine inference engine into smaller, independently testable sub-modules within NGHE Solution (e.g., phase-transition validator, edge-case handler, timestamp-anchor resolver). Document phase transition rules, guard conditions, and validation criteria in a formal specification.

Define explicit semantics for CT_target and statistical parameter updates via the Tasking interface. Specify whether updates apply to the next cycle, drain the current rolling buffer, or merge with existing statistics. Document in the Tasking interface contract.

Implement efficient circular buffer or time-windowed persistence for the audit trail to prevent storage bottlenecks at high cycle frequencies. Consider offloading historical audit data to the Network Link for archival and long-term retention.

Add a validation checkpoint in NGHE Solution to confirm that measured p95 percentile is computed over a representative sample size (e.g., minimum number of cycles) before comparing against CT_target. Document minimum sample size requirements in the acceptance criteria.

Ensure that the audit trail includes sufficient metadata (sensor IDs, timestamps, inferred phase sequence, raw telemetry snapshots) to enable root-cause analysis of outliers. Define audit trail schema and retention policy in the module design.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. Verification feasibility is high, allocation clarity is excellent, and containment is strong (0.92 score). However, the recommended revisions and diffusion risks reveal that the current FR-DP-IM specification, while logically sound, requires decomposition into lower-level sub-FRs to address: (1) time-synchronization protocol enforcement, (2) state-machine inference robustness and testability, (3) parameter update semantics clarity, (4) audit trail storage efficiency, and (5) sample-size validation for statistical acceptance. These are not defects in the current level but rather implementation concerns that benefit from explicit functional decomposition. Given high safety criticality, deriving sub-FRs ensures that critical concerns (time-sync, state-machine correctness, audit integrity) are elevated to first-class functional requirements with their own DP-IM mappings and verification criteria. This approach prevents these concerns from being buried in implementation details and ensures they receive appropriate design rigor and testing.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Establish and validate event timestamp synchronization between Vehicle sensors and NGHE Solution to ensure reliable phase-boundary anchoring within acceptable clock-skew tolerance.
FR5.2: Infer loadâ†’travelâ†’dump cycle phases from discrete event sensors and continuous telemetry with formal state-machine logic that is independently testable and handles edge-case transitions.
FR5.3: Aggregate cycle-time measurements into a rolling statistical buffer and compute p95 percentile over a representative sample size (minimum cycle count) to enable reliable performance reporting.
FR5.4: Maintain an audit trail linking each measured cycle to raw sensor data, inferred phase sequence, and computed metrics to enable root-cause analysis of outliers and performance anomalies.

FR5.5: Apply CT_target and statistical parameter updates via Tasking interface with explicit semantics (e.g., apply-to-next-cycle, drain-buffer, merge-with-existing) to prevent rolling-buffer inconsistency.

revise.why: No revisions needed at current level. FR5, DP5, and IM (NGHE Solution) are sound and well-integrated. Decomposition is the appropriate next step.

assumptions: Vehicle sensors provide discrete event signals (load-start, travel-start, dump-start, dump-end) with timestamps that can be synchronized to NGHE Solution clock within acceptable tolerance (e.g., Â±100 ms).
Continuous telemetry (position, velocity, load-cell readings) is available at sufficient sampling rate (â‰Ą10 Hz) to refine phase boundaries and detect edge cases.
State-machine inference logic can be formally specified and decomposed into independently testable sub-modules without introducing unacceptable latency or computational overhead.
Audit trail storage and retrieval can be implemented with circular buffering and time-windowed persistence without exceeding NGHE Solution memory or I/O budgets.
CT_target and statistical parameters are updated infrequently (e.g., once per shift or per site reconfiguration) such that update semantics can be clearly defined and validated.

Safety Services and Teleoperation clients can tolerate the latency and eventual-consistency semantics of the rolling statistical buffer (i.e., p95 percentile is computed over a sliding window, not a fixed epoch).
open_risks: Time-sync protocol failure: If Vehicle sensors and NGHE Solution clocks drift beyond tolerance, event anchoring will fail and phase inference will be unreliable. Mitigation: Implement continuous clock-skew monitoring and alert if tolerance is exceeded.

State-machine complexity creep: As cycle phases proliferate or edge cases accumulate, inference logic becomes difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: Enforce strict phase taxonomy and document all guard conditions; use formal methods (e.g., state-machine verification tools) to validate correctness.

Audit trail storage bottleneck: High cycle frequency (e.g., 100+ cycles/day) and long retention periods (e.g., 1 year) may cause I/O or memory pressure. Mitigation: Implement tiered storage (hot buffer, warm archive, cold long-term) and monitor storage utilization.

Parameter update race condition: Concurrent updates to CT_target or statistical parameters while rolling buffer is being computed may leave buffer in inconsistent state. Mitigation: Use atomic update semantics (e.g., copy-on-write, versioned parameters) and document in Tasking interface contract.

Sample-size validation gap: If p95 percentile is computed over too few cycles (e.g., <10), statistical confidence is low. Mitigation: Enforce minimum sample-size threshold before accepting p95 as valid; document in acceptance criteria.

Audit trail metadata insufficiency: If audit trail lacks raw sensor snapshots or inferred phase sequence, root-cause analysis of outliers will be difficult. Mitigation: Define comprehensive audit trail schema and validate completeness at module boundary.
evidence_needed: Time-sync protocol specification: Define acceptable clock-skew tolerance, synchronization mechanism (e.g., NTP, PTP, GPS), and validation checks at Telemetry interface.
State-machine formal specification: Document all cycle phases, transition rules, guard conditions, and edge-case handling logic. Provide state-transition diagram and formal verification results.
Audit trail schema and retention policy: Define audit trail data structure, metadata fields, storage format, retention period, and archival strategy.

Parameter update semantics specification: Define how CT_target and statistical parameter updates are applied (e.g., apply-to-next-cycle, drain-buffer, merge-with-existing) and document in Tasking interface contract.
Sample-size validation criteria: Define minimum number of cycles required for p95 percentile to be considered statistically valid. Provide justification based on statistical confidence requirements.
Storage capacity analysis: Estimate audit trail storage requirements at expected cycle frequency and retention period. Validate that NGHE Solution storage budget is sufficient.

Integration test plan: Define test scenarios for time-sync validation, state-machine edge cases, parameter updates, and audit trail completeness. Include failure-mode testing (e.g., clock drift, sensor dropout, parameter conflicts).
